text: Laparoscopic splenectomy with selective intracorporeal ligation of splenic hilar vessels for high-grade splenic injury. A fifty-nine years old lady is admitted after a domestic fall with blunt left-sided thoracobdominal trauma. Patient is awake, euthanized and observations are stable.

CT scan thorax and abdomen with IV contrast shows left lung contusions and multiple left ribs fractures with no pneumothorax. High-grade splenic injury with intraparenchymal hematoma and small arterial blashes in the inferior splenic pole. Associated with celiac trunk stenosis. Mild intradominal free fluid and grade II injury to the left kidney.

Ungymbolization attempt failed because of celiac trunk stenosis. However, because of patient's history of chronic psychosis with opioids and alcohol abuse, as well as HCV-positive liver disease with splenomegaly, and living alone, numb without ungymbolization was considered contraindicated and not safe in such patient. In addition, non-without tangibilization would have required a long length of stay for observation.

Being the patient hemodynamically stable, laparoscopic splenectomy was feasible and would have allowed a faster resume to daily life. Pros and cons were discussed with the patient that decided for laparoscopic splenectomy.

The patient is positioned in a right semilateral decubitus position. Pneumoperitoneum is established in the right upper quadrant, and four additional trocars are placed in standard positions. The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to divide the adhesions, exposing the inferior splenic pole with ultramatic graspers. The spleen is gently lifted with a blunt retractor, revealing a large intraparenchymal hematoma without free fluid.

The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to divide the splenocolic ligament. Minor bleeding from vessels or the splenic capsule is controlled with bipolar energy. The division of the splenocolic ligament is completed.

The surgeon tilts the spleen medially to expose the splenorenal ligament, which is divided using a monopolar hook. Subsequently, the lateral side of the splenophrenic ligament is divided with the same instrument, taking care to avoid diaphragmatic injury and potential pneumothorax.

The surgeon tilts the spleen laterally to expose the splenic hilum, gastrosplenic ligament, and medial side of the splenophrenic ligament. The splenophrenic ligament is divided using a monopolar hook. The splenic hilum is approached from caudal to cranial by lifting the inferior splenic pole with a blunt retractor, and the peritoneal surface of the hilum is opened using the hook, look, and cook technique with a monopolar hook.

The surgeon uses bipolar energy to safely control and divide minor vessels, followed by hook dissection to expose the splenic hilar vessels. Major vessels are carefully dissected and prepared using Maryland and right angle forceps, with no harmonic device used throughout the procedure.

The surgeon identifies the splenic main artery, which appears smaller due to celiac trunk stenosis. A haemolog clip is applied to the proximal side of the vessel, followed by securing the proximal end with an intracorporeal tie.

Laparoscopy has several advantages when compared to open approach. In fact, it allows a faster postoperative recovery, reduced pain, wound and chest infections and incisional hernias. The reduced rate of chest infection of laparoscopy is particularly important in trauma patients with lung contusions and rib fractures.

The surgeon places an intracorporeal tie on the distal side of the splenic vessel to allow space for quick stapling or clamping of the hilum in case of sudden major bleeding. The vessel is then sharply divided.

The surgeon uses Maryland forceps and a hook to dissect and expose the inferior splenic pole vessels. The inferior polar vein and artery are clearly visible and are subsequently clipped, tied, and sharply divided.

The surgeon observes transient bleeding from a large hematoma due to increased intraparenchymal pressure following the ligation of a major splenic vein. The bleeding is managed by packing and ceases once the main splenic artery is clipped.

The surgeon continues the hilar dissection using a monopolar hook and bipolar energy, dissecting, clipping, and sharply dividing a branch of the main splenic artery and the splenic hilar vein. With the major splenic vessels already ligated, hemo-locks are placed on both sides to prevent bleeding. The hyaluronate division is completed safely using monopolar and bipolar cautery.

The surgeon uses a vascular endostapler to divide the short gastric vessels, ensuring hemostasis by waiting one minute after closing the stapler jaws before firing. Bipolar energy is applied to refine hemostasis.

The surgeon retrieves the splenectomy specimen using a large endo bag, extracting it through the left upper quadrant incision by morcellation without enlarging the wound. The surgical field is cleaned and inspected, with careful checking of hemostasis at the splenic hilum and short gastric vessels. Haemostatic sealant is applied to ensure adequate hemostasis.

This video shows how laparoscopic splenectomy with selective ligation of highly vessels in selected patients with high-grade splenic trauma is safe and feasible provided the presence of advanced laparoscopic skills. Postoperative recovery was uneventful. On past operative day four, the patient was transferred to a rehabilitation facility for social discharge because she was living alone in her medical comorbidities.
